feedback.text: Overall, I support the initiative. However, I would like the Act to be expanded to digital goods purchased. Too many companies use the words "Buy"/"Purchase"/etc, and then hide behind EULAs and TOSes that redefine those words to mean "License", including a one-sided ability to take away access. While in certain situations, certain aspects of products might be necessarily taken away (hackers in online games being an obvious one), quite often it's used a clutch to reduce costs on the business. An example would be when Sony one-sidedly removed shows owned by Discovery from people's libraries (https://www.businessinsider.com/playstation-removes-hundreds-of-purchased-discovery-shows-from-library-2023-12), or when game developers pull access from people for certain games (Ubisoft: The Crew (https://www.ign.com/articles/ubisofts-the-crew-lawsuit-insists-we-do-not-own-the-games-we-buy), Activision-Blizzard: Overwatch and Valve: Counter-Strike Global Offensive (both cases, they released a "sequel" that replaced the original game, but with increased system requirements which made the games unavailable to many original owners of the games)). While this is not a common occurrence **yet**, it is getting more and more widespread with Digital Media. I can watch a DVD I bought in 1998, I can play games I bought physically in 2012, and it's not possible to prevent me from being able to do so. Digital Media bought in 2025 can be taken away the literal same day in certain cases with no compensation and no protection from the law, with no warranty, and no recourse, legal or otherwise.
